title: Cosmoplex: self-consistent simulation of self-organizing systems
abstract: A method for simulation, in particular for computerized calculation, of at least one physical property of a system of one or more chemical species that includes at least one chemical species dissolved in at least one inhomogeneously distributed chemical species, using a quasi-chemical calculation based on the statistical thermodynamics of pairwise interactions of molecule surface segments. According to the invention, pressure, that arises from the statistical thermodynamic over- or underpopulation of spatial regions, interacts as an additional continuum response function with the atomic volumes and thus influences the thermodynamic weight of a molecular state in the system, during the iterative calculation of the statistical thermodynamic distribution of molecules in 1-dimensionally, 2-dimensionally, or 3-dimensionally structured simulation volumes of liquid systems.
claims: Claims P a t e n t c l a i m s 1. A method (100) for simulation, in particular for computerized calculation, of at least one physical property of a system of one or more chemical species that includes at least one chemical species dissolved in at least one inhomogeneously distributed chemical species, using a quasi-chemical calculation based on the statistical thermodynamics of pairwise interactions of molecular surface segments, characterized in that pressure, that arises from the statistical thermodynamic over- or underpopulation of spatial regions, interacts as an additional continuum response function with the atomic volumes and thus influences the thermodynamic weight of a molecular state in the system, during the iterative calculation of the statistical thermodynamic distribution of molecules in 1 - dimensionally, 2-dimensionally, or 3-dimensionally structured simulation volumes of liquid systems. 2. The method (100) according to claim 1 , characterized in that the pressure is used as an additional energy contribution in the thermodynamic weight of a molecular state in the system with the energy contributions, that arise from the interactions of molecular surfaces by the quasi-chemical calculation. 3. The method (100) according to claim 1 or to claim 2, wherein the structure of the chemical species can be self-consistently calculated within the same approach, avoiding the need for external information. 4. The method (100) according to any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that a pressure profile arises from the statistical thermodynamic over- or underpopulation of spatial regions, is used. 5. The method (100) according to any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that the simulated chemical system is a micelle or a membrane. 6. The method (100) according to any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that pure or mixed surfactant systems and biomembranes with finite load of solutes can be calculated. 7. The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that ionic surfactans with self-consistent ion distribution can be calculated. 8. The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that molecules at liquid-liquid interfaces and interfacial tensions can be calculated. 9. The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that molecules at liquid-gas interfaces and surface tensions can be calculated. 10. The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that molecules at liquid-solid interfaces and adsorption constants can be calculated. 1 1 . The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that confined liquids can be calculated. 12. The method (100) according to any one of the claims 1 to 5, characterized in that liquid crystals can be calculated. 13. The method (100) according to any one of the preceding claims comprising computer implemented steps, in particular wherein the simulation is carried out by computerized calculation. 14. A computer apparatus for simulation, preferably for computerized calculation, of at least one physical property of a system of one or more chemical species that includes at least one chemical species dissolved in at least one inhomogeneously distributed chemical species, using a quasi-chemical calculation involving calculation of the pressure, that arises from the statistical thermodynamic over- or underpopulation of spatial regions, preferably according to the method of at least one of the preceding claims 1 to 13.
